title: I Started Quoting Faster as a Handyman — Then I Built the App
meta_title: Send a Quote On Site in ~30 Seconds | Handyman Founder Story
meta_description: I run Good Hands Handyman. I tried AI to estimate faster and it guessed my prices. Here’s how I send an on-site quote in about 30 seconds with my own rates — and why first quote wins.
excerpt: I’m still on the tools. I didn’t build SMASH from a boardroom. I built it because “I’ll email a quote tonight” was losing me jobs — and ChatGPT was guessing my rates.
key_takeaways: I didn’t need prettier quote templates — I needed a number in the customer’s hand before I left.
Generic AI estimated faster, then got my labour and materials wrong.
The fix is your catalog in the background, then voice on top for speed.
Same talk → verify → send loop becomes the invoice when the job is done.

faq_data.question: Why do on-site quotes win more jobs?
faq_data.answer: Because the customer is deciding while you’re still there. Whoever puts a clear priced PDF in their hand first usually wins — not the bloke who emails a perfect spreadsheet at 9pm.

faq_data.question: Can AI write a handyman quote for you?
faq_data.answer: It can draft wording. It does not know your call-out fee, your hourly rate, or what you charged last time unless you retype it every session. That’s why guesses feel fast and still cost you money.

faq_data.question: How do you send a quote in about 30 seconds?
faq_data.answer: Talk the scope out loud against your saved prices, verify the lines, send the PDF before you start the van. That’s the loop I built into SMASH after doing it the hard way.

faq_data.question: What happens after they approve the quote?
faq_data.answer: Do the job, convert the quote to an invoice, adjust only what changed, send before you leave. Don’t rebuild the whole thing from memory on Sunday.

author: Dan Neale
author_bio: Dan Neale runs Good Hands Handyman and founded SMASH Invoices. He’s dyslexic, still on the tools, and built voice quoting because typing estimates was losing him work.
featured_image_alt: Handyman founder sending an on-site quote from iPhone
content:
## Short answer

I’m a working handyman. I didn’t start SMASH because I loved “AI invoicing.” I started it because **I needed estimates faster** — and every shortcut I tried got the money wrong.

Now I send an **on-site quote in about 30 seconds**: talk the job, verify my prices, send the PDF before I leave. First clear number usually wins.

Product: [Voice quote / invoice on iPhone](/voice-invoicing). My story: [Meet the founder](/founder).

## The real problem wasn’t typing speed

I run [Good Hands Handyman](https://www.goodhandshandyman.com.au). I’m still on jobs. I’m also dyslexic — sitting down to type a neat quote after a long day was slow and painful. So I did what everyone does now: I looked at AI.

Paste the job into ChatGPT. Ask for a professional quote. It sounded great.

Then I checked the numbers.

Wrong hourly rate. Soft materials. Call-out fee missing. GST vibes instead of *my* GST. It was writing an essay about the job, not quoting **my** business.

That’s the trap: **fast wording, wrong money.**

## Why “I’ll email a quote tonight” loses

On site, someone asks “how much?”

If I say I’ll send it later, I’m competing with whoever already answered. I learned that the hard way. The first priced document in their hand wins more often than the perfect one that never leaves the drafts folder.

That’s why I care about [first quote wins — instant quote on site](/blog/first-quote-wins-instant-quote-on-site) more than another template pack.

## What I built instead

I didn’t want a chatbot that invents prices. I wanted:

1. **My rates and materials in the background** — a catalog that remembers what I charge
2. **A verify step** — unmatched items flagged, not silently guessed
3. **Voice on top** — because talking is how I already describe jobs to mates and customers

Voice without catalog is just dictation. Catalog without speed is still Sunday night. Together it’s talk → verify → send.

Same loop for invoices when the work is done: [what is voice to invoice?](/blog/what-is-voice-to-invoice).

## The ~30-second on-site quote, the way I actually do it

1. **Talk the scope** — rooms, labour, fittings, call-out if it applies.
2. **Glance the lines** — quantities and prices from *my* list.
3. **Send** — branded PDF while I’m still in the driveway.

If they approve and I finish the job, I convert to invoice. I don’t rebuild it from memory at the kitchen table.

Desk requests in email? Different surface, same idea: [Gmail invoice](/gmail-invoice).

## If you’re already pasting jobs into ChatGPT

You’re not lazy. You’re trying to move faster. I did the same thing.

Just know what ChatGPT is good at (wording) and what it isn’t (your catalog, sendable tax docs, payment). Longer take: [Can ChatGPT write a quote or estimate?](/blog/can-chatgpt-write-a-quote-estimate) and [Can ChatGPT generate invoices?](/blog/can-chatgpt-generate-invoices-tradie-guide).

## Bottom line

I built SMASH for me — a handyman who needed **estimates quicker without wrong prices**. Voice is the accelerator. The catalog is the truth.
